articles.title: about this blog
articles.contents:
Hello.
I’m currently a doctoral student in Sociology at Duke University.
My research interests have to do with culture, economic sociology, and network theory.
I’m also interested in contributing to the type of quantitative research that fights against this common misconception:



Currently thinking about:
When do “formal structures” work and when do they fail? (e.g., Scott 1998; Stinchcombe 2001)
The evolution of legal precedent from the perspective of endogenous cultural change (Kaufman 2004)
How political elites shape belief networks.
Networks of social support (e.g., Small 2017), kinship, and gender.
Factor analysis and other dimension reduction techniques (e.g., Bartholomew, Knott, and Moustaki 2011; Holmes 2008; Breiger 2000; Blei 2014)
Updated: May 05, 2021



Bartholomew, David J., Martin Knott, and Irini Moustaki. 2011. Latent Variable Models and Factor Analysis: A Unified Approach. Vol. 904. John Wiley & Sons.


Blei, David M. 2014. “Build, Compute, Critique, Repeat: Data Analysis with Latent Variable Models.” Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application 1: 203232.


Breiger, Ronald L. 2000. “A Tool Kit for Practice Theory.” Poetics 27 (2-3): 91115.


Holmes, Susan. 2008. “Multivariate Data Analysis: The French Way.” In, 219233. Institute of Mathematical Statistics.


Kaufman, Jason. 2004. “Endogenous Explanation in the Sociology of Culture.” Annu. Rev. Sociol. 30: 335357.


Scott, James C. 1998. Seeing Like a State: How Certain Schemes to Improve the Human Condition Have Failed. New Haven: Yale University Press.


Small, Mario Luis. 2017. Someone to Talk to. Oxford University Press.


Stinchcombe, Arthur L. 2001. When Formality Works: Authority and Abstraction in Law and Organizations. University of Chicago Press.
